Ref. Gough, C.E., 2016. “Violin Acoustics”. Acoustics Today 12, 22–30.

title: Lecture 12: Solid Bodies
section: Vibration modes of a solid body
text:
Vibration modes of a solid body



Transverse: like a string
Longitudinal: stretch and compress
Torsional: twisting

For most solid bodies the transverse modes are the dominant ones.




Types of vibrational modes in solids: (a) transverse (b) longitudinal (c) torsional

title: Lecture 12: Solid Bodies
text:
Bars, rods, tubes


These are generally percussion instruments that are struck with a mallet: glockenspiel, xylophone, chimes.
The fundamental mode is a transverse vibration. If the width of the bar is w and its length is ℓ, then the fundamental frequency goes like \[
f_1 = k \frac{w}{\ell^2},
\] where k is a number that depends on the material properties and the specifics of the geometry (round, square, etc.).

title: Lecture 12: Solid Bodies
text:
Modes of bars


The vibrational modes of long bars look similar to the modes of strings, but the loops are not of equal length.
This makes the partials inharmonic. If the fundamental mode has frequency f1, then the next few modes are 2.76f1, 5.40f1, 8.93f1, 13.34f1.
The bars in glockenspiel, marimbas, etc., are mounted at the nodes of the fundamental, a little outside of 1/4 of the way from the ends, in order to suppress the higher partials.

title: Lecture 12: Solid Bodies
text:
Reeds


Here we’re talking about reeds that vibrate at their natural frequency, like in an accordion or harmonica, not a reed in a woodwind instrument.
The difference between a “reed” and a “bar” is that one end of a reed is clamped or held in place as it vibrates.
The fundamental mode is a transverse vibration, just like bars, and the equation is the same. If the thickness of the reed is t and its length is ℓ, then the fundamental frequency goes like \[
f_1 = k \frac{t}{\ell^2},
\] where k is a number that depends on the material properties.




Lowest 3 modes of a vibrating reed



The next two modes are at \(f_2 = 6.25 f_1\) and \(f_3 = 17.5 f_1\)

title: Lecture 12: Solid Bodies
text:
Plates and membranes


These are both thin flat objects. The details of the vibrational structures depend on the details of the shape how the object is mounted.
The bodies of string instruments are plates in this sense, as well as things like cymbals and gongs.
There aren’t any general equations for the frequencies, so you have to look on a case-by-case basis.




Chladni plates mounded at the center





Drumhead modes

text: Daily Quiz

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Definitions of Intensity and Loudness

Intensity is a measure of how much sound energy passes through a surface in a certain amount of time (power per area).

This is an objective description of sound that we can measure with a machine (e.g. with a sound meter).

Loudness (or “volume”) is the human perception of sound intensity.

It is a subjective description of sound as heard by each listener.
Dynamics markings in a musical score (forte, piano, etc.) indicate loudness.
Because each listener may hear something different (e.g. because of age or medical condition), loudness is harder to describe precisely.
We’ll come back to loudness in the next chapter to talk about how an average adult human perceives intensity and the different factors that affect loudness.

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Energy, Power, and Intensity



Energy (E) is the capacity to do work. It is measured as joules (J), which the book also calls newton-meters (N m).
Power (P) is the rate at which energy is emitted or absorbed. Power is measured in watts (W), which is also joules per seconds.
Intensity (I) is the amount of power divided by the area it passes through, measured in watts per square-meter (W/m2).

\[P = IA\]

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Decibels
To avoid writing very small and very large numbers (lots of zeros or decimals points) to represent quantities with a lot of dynamics range, engineers often use a logarithmic scale called decibels (dB) to express ratios.
The mathematical definition of decibels is
\[\text{decibels} = 10 \log\left(\frac{\text{measurement value}}{\text{reference value}}\right),\]
where “log” is the common (base-10) logarithm function found on a calculator.
If we’re given the decibel level and we need the measurement value, we solve the equation above to get
\[\text{measurement} = \text{reference value} \times 10^{\text{decibels}/10} \]

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Sound intensity levels (SIL)


The reference value for a decibel conversion is usually something agreed upon by the community. For example, when measuring sound intensity, the reference value is taken as the threshold of hearing for a typical adult human: I0 = 10-12 W/m2, giving us the following definition for Sound Intensity Level (SIL), measured in decibels:
\[S_{IL} = 10 \log(I/I_0)\]
or, flipping this around to solve for intensity:
\[I = I_0 \times 10^{S_{IL}/10},\]
where for sound, I0 = 10-12 W/m2 by definition.




Perceived intensity vs. frequency

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Pressure
We need the link between changes in air pressure and the sound intensity to define the various measures of loudness in this chapter.
Pressure is the force per unit area applied to a surface. It has units of newtons per square-meter, also called “pascals” (Pa). Standard atmospheric air pressure is 101325 Pa. Sound waves in air are a very small change in this number.
Pressure change (p) is a useful description of sound because most sound-detection devices use pressure as the mechanism to do the detection.

Our eardrums move because of the air pressure fluctuations, which then move the small bones in the middle ear.
Most microphones use a similar effect, converting pressure to an electrical signal (voltage or current).

The smallest pressure change that humans can hear is called p0 and equals
\[ p_0 = 2\times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{Pa} = 20 \mathrm{\mu Pa}.\]

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Pressure, intensity, impedance
Last time we used intensity (I) as our measure of sound. The relationship between intensity and pressure depends on details like the material and geometric details, which we call the acoustic impedance (z).
The units of impedance are pascals-seconds per meter (Pa s/m), also called “rayl”.
If the impedance is known, intensity and pressure are related by the equation
\[ I = \frac{p^2}{z} \]

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Nominal acoustic impedance
For air at room temperature and pressure and in a large room away from any walls, the value of this impedance is called z0:
\[ z_0 = 413\,\mathrm{rayl} \]
By comparison, the impedance of water at room temperature is about zwater=1.5×106 rayl.

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Example: Thresholds of hearing
Recall from last time that the minimum sound intensity a typical adult human can hear is I0=10-12 W/m2 at 1 kHz. This compare this to what we get from the minimum pressure (p0=2×10-5 Pa) and the impedance of air (z0=413 Pa s/m).
\[\frac{p_0^2}{z_0} = \frac{(2\times10^{-5}\,\mathrm{Pa})^2}{413\,\mathrm{Pa\,s/m}} = 9.7\times10^{-13}\,\mathrm{W/m^2}.\]
Rounding this up we get 1×10-12 W/m2, which is the value we called I0.

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
More decibels
From last time our conversion from intensity I to sound intensity level in decibels (SIL) was
\[ S_{IL} = (10\,\mathrm{dB}) \log\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right).\]
There is also a decibel measure for pressure, called sound pressure level (SPL) with a slightly different formula:
\[ S_{PL} = (20\,\mathrm{dB})\log\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right).\]
(Notice the twenty here instead of ten in the top equation.)

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
SPL or SIL?
All of this gets confusing, but the important thing is that in air SIL and SPL have the same number value. For most of our applications, they can be used interchangeably.
So why do we have both? When sound travels from one medium to another (or through complicated shapes), the pressure is the same everywhere even though the intensity is not. This is the mathematical basis for impedance mismatches.
The pressure must stay the same because of Newton’s 3rd Law of Motion (action/reaction), but the intensity going across the boundary is determined by the impedance formula. Intensity must still be conserved (it’s energy), so the intensity that doesn’t make it across the boundary gets reflected out.

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Phons


A phon (rhymes with “Don”) is a unit of perceived intensity, as determined by experiments.
Last time I showed this graph relating sound intensity to the limits of human hearing for different frequencies.
The red curve was the lowest sound that can be heard at that frequency.
Phon is the sound level relative to that red line, measured in decibels. So, 0 phons is the loudness of points exactly on the red line. 20 phons is an intensity at the bottom blue line, and so on up the chart.




Fletcher–Munson loudness curves of perceived intensity vs. frequency(see also, textbook Figure 10-1)

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Adding several sources (same frequency)
The book makes this more confusing than it needs to be. The rule is simple

When combining multiple sound sources of the same frequency: add the intensities, not the sound levels (not the decibels).

So, if given the levels in decibels (either SIL or SPL), convert to intensity, add the intensities, then convert back to decibels.
If you have N identical sources, then their combined sound level will be the level of one source (in dB) plus (10 log N).

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Sound subjective loudness (Sones)


Subjective loudness (SSL) is measured in sones (rhymes with “bones”). The sone tries to capture the idea of what it means for one sound to be “twice as loud” as another sound.
The basic relationship is that multiplying the sound intensity by 10 (equivalently, adding 10 dB to the sound level or adding 10 phons) creates a sound that seems 2 times as loud to an average adult human. The baseline is 1 sone = 40 phons.

Phon was the more “scientific” description and sone the more “musical” description: doubling the sone value is roughly the same as increasing by one musical dynamic marking.

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Example problem
A piccolo plays the pitch C6 (about 1046 Hz) at a forte dynamic.
What are the values of

the sound subjective loudness (SSL) in sones?
the sound loudness level (SLL) in phons?
the sound intensity level (SIL) in decibels?
the sound pressure level (SPL) in decibels?
the sound intensity (I) in W/m2?
the sound pressure change (p) in Pa?

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Review: Beat notes
A few lectures ago we showed what happens when you have two tones with slightly different frequencies.
If two similar frequencies of similar intensity are heard at the same time:

the listener will hear the average frequency,
which will modulate in loudness with a beat frequency equal to the difference in the two original frequencies.

But we know from experience that if the two frequencies are far enough apart, instead of beats we will hear harmony. Where is that dividing line?

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Critical band


Because of the way the cochlea in the ear works, frequencies very close to each other will excite the same nerve cells in the ear. The frequency range than any particular nerve in the ear can detect is called the critical band around that frequency.

If two tones are within the same critical band, then we will perceive beat notes.
If two tones are separated by more than the critical band, then we perceive two distinct tones.

If the two tones are played in different ears you don’t hear beats!




W&W Fig. 10-3: Critical bands


The top solid curve is the difference in frequency needed to distinguish two tones clearly.

title: Lecture 9: Loudness
text:
Masking


The concept of masking is similar to the critical bands. Loud tones can hide quiet tones that are close in frequency.
If we increase the loudness of the quiet tone, eventually it will break through. This point is called the “threshold increase”.




W&W Fig. 10-6: Masking curves for an 800-Hz tone

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Definition of Interference
Unlike the book, I’m going to discuss interference first because we can then use these ideas to talk about diffraction.
Interference is the adding of waves with the same frequency/wavelength.

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Principle of Superposition
When two or more waves overlap at the same place, we add their amplitudes at that point.

Flipping Physics demo: https://youtu.be/JUCLoczzwPo
Slinky demo: https://youtu.be/X2pt9WCu8mk 
CCC demo: https://youtu.be/hnZ1FKVWN4k

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text: Constructive and Destructive Interference

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Phase difference
Phase is a measure of time difference between two waves. We measure it in “waves” or as an angle, with 1 wave = 360°.



Completely constructive interference happens at a phase difference of 0 waves or 0°.
Completely destructive interference happens at a phase difference of 1/2 wave or 180°.
In between we have partial interference.
The pattern repeats after each whole wave or each 360°.

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Calculating phase difference


To calculate the phase difference between two sources we do:

Measure the distance from each source to the listener (call these d1 and d2).
Divide the difference in distances by the wavelength (λ), then multiply by 360°: \(\phi = \frac{d_1 - d_2}{\lambda}\times 360^\circ\)
In these drawings, the wavelength is λ = 1 m.





Constructive





Destructive

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text: Interference in other waves

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text: Noise cancellation

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Definition of Diffraction
I’m going to give a different definition of diffraction than the book, but one that I think makes more sense.
Diffraction is the interference of a wave with itself.
All of the math we used for interference applies here, too, if we look at the wave in the right way.

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Hugyen’s Principle


Huygens’s Principle is the idea that we can treat each point along a wavefront as its own point source. Looks for the constructive interference of these imaginary point sources let’s use determine the shape of the wave as it propagates.

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Diffraction compared to wavelength
Diffraction happens when waves pass through a hole that is similar in size to the wavelength.

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Diffraction from a slit


For a slit or rectangular hole, the sound is concentrated in a cone with angular width θ given by
\[\sin\theta = \frac{\lambda}{D}\]
where λ is the wavelength and D is the size of the hole. Solving for the angle we have
\[\theta = \sin^{-1}\left( \frac{\lambda}{D}\right)\]

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
PhET demo
Demo on wave interference and diffraction: https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/wave-interference/latest/wave-interference_en.html

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Diffraction from a circular hole


For a circular hole, the result is similar but the formula has an extra factor of 1.22:
\[\sin\theta = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D}\]
where λ is the wavelength and D is the diameter of the hole. Solving for the angle we have
\[\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D}\right)\]

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Phased array


We can find the diffraction patterns of other shapes by going back to Huygen’s Principle of treating each point as a new source. With proper engineering we can control both the amplitude and phase of each point of the new wave.
For example, if we set up a line of speakers and control the phase of each, we have a phased array. This can be used to control the direction of sound emitted by the speakers (“beam steering”).
Near the source, the wave is mixed up, but after a few wavelengths, the shape becomes apparent.

title: Lecture 6: Diffraction and Interference
text:
Diffusors
For example, some acoustic diffusors are actually diffraction devices.

The complex patterns here cause the sound to scatter in quasi-random directions because of diffraction. Each hard surface acts like an individual source of sound. The complex pattern mixes up the sound so that it doesn’t reflect directly.
Video: https://youtu.be/1MtJOGXVZ1w

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Pitch
Pitch is the musical term for the identity of a musical tone in terms of its fundamental partial. The identification of pitch is a rich topic itself with lots of ties to history and culture.
We’re going to keep a very basic definition of pitch and define it as the way we distinguish keys on a piano.

One octave of a piano keyboard looks like the picture here. The white keys are given names in a repeating pattern using letters A through G, and the black keys are named equally as the neighboring white key to the left plus “sharp” (♯) or the white key to the right plus “flat” (♭). The fact that, for example, B♭ and A♯ are the same key is called enharmonicity, but as we’ll see later, this is because of our choice of the way the piano is tuned.

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Octaves on the piano keys
The pattern of 7 white keys and 5 black keys repeats in sets called “octaves.” Each octave has 12 keys. We’ll label the octaves with a number. By tradition, the C closest to the middle of the piano keyboard is called “middle C” and we’ll give it the name C4. The next C to the left on the keyboard (“lower”) we call C3, and the C to the right of middle C (“higher”) is called C5.
Each new octave number begins at C. The key to the left of C4 is called B3.

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Absolute pitch


By international treaty(!!) the key A4 on the piano (above middle C) is tuned such that its fundamental partial has a frequency of 440 Hz. This is called the “standard concert pitch”. There is a rich history of the various disputes around this value (mostly political soap operas between various nations, orchestras, and composers, see Wikipedia), even making an appearance in the Treaty of Versailles that ended World War I.
The modern value is attributed to German physicist Johann Scheibler who presented the first reliable measurements of musical frequencies at a conference in Stuttgart in 1834, with the value A4 = 440 Hz first being recognized as an international standard in 1939 and then reaffirmed in 1975.

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Octaves


Going back to our discussion of vibrating strings, we saw that the fundamental frequency of the string was given by \[
f_1 = \frac{1}{2L}\,\sqrt{\frac{F}{m}},
\] and that the first overtone is at exactly 2 times this frequency: \(f_2 = 2f_1\).
We say that two frequencies with this relationship are an octave apart. Or, in terms of their ratios: \[
\text{octave} = 2:1
\]
When played together, human hearing will interpret these as the same pitch.

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Octaves of A’s


If we take as a starting point A4 = 440 Hz, we can use our definition of octave to calculate the frequencies of all of the other A’s on the piano keyboard by successively multiplying or dividing by two.
This is called a geometric sequence in math. Rather than adding or subtracting to go from one pitch to the next, we multiply or divide.
To find the frequencies of the other pitches, we need a mathematical rule. Historically, many different rules, called temperments or tunings were used. We’ll start with the most common modern rule: equal temperment.

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Equal temperament
The rule for the 12-tone equal tempered scale (12-TET) simply says that the ratio of frequencies of adjacent keys on the piano must be the same for every pair of keys.
Let’s do a little algebra. If the ratio between adjacent keys is called x, then after 12 keys we should have an octave, or two times the starting frequency: \[
f_1 \times x^{12} = 2f_1
\] Solving for x gives the ratio between adjacent keys: \[
x = \sqrt[12]{2} \approx 1.059463\ldots
\] In words, the ratio is the twelfth root of 2. We can also write this as \(2^{1/12}\),2 to the 1/12th power, which is easier to do algebra with.
This interval is called a semitone of the equal-tempered scale.

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Examples


The frequency of A♯4 is \[
A^\sharp_4 = A_4\times 2^{1/12} = 440\,\mathrm{Hz}\times 2^{1/12} = 466.2\,\mathrm{Hz}.
\]


To get A♭4 we divide by \(\sqrt[12]{2}\): \[
A^\flat_4 = \frac{A_4}{2^{1/12}} = 415.3\,\mathrm{Hz}
\]


To get C5, we step up by 3 keys on the keyboard: \[
C_5 = A_4 \times (2^{1/12})^3  = A_4\times 2^{1/4} = 440\,\mathrm{Hz}\times1.189=523\,\mathrm{Hz}
\]

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Full equal tempered chromatic scale

Stepping one note at a time across the keyboard (including the black keys), we get the full 12-tone chromatic scale.
Again, the defining characteristic of the equal tempered scale is that the frequency ratios of adjacent notes is the same: \(2^{1/2} \approx 1.059463\).

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Cents
Because multiplying and dividing frequency numbers is inconvenient, we often characterize pitch in terms of a measure called cents that we can add or subtract. Cents live on a logarithmic scale, such that adding 1200 cents doubles the frequency. Adding or subtracting 100 cents moves us by one semitone (one piano key) up or down on the equal tempered scale.
If we have two frequencies, their difference in cents from f1 to f2 is given by \[
\mathrm{cents} = 1200 \log_2 (f_2/f_1).
\] Where “log2” means the base-2 logarithm. Most calculators can’t do this directly, so using some math rules for logs we get the equation \[
\mathrm{cents} = \frac{1200}{\log 2} \log (f_2/f_1)
\approx 3986 \log(f_1/f_2).
\]
(This should remind you of decibels. The math is very similar.)

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Cents to frequency
Going the other way, if we know f1 and want to change by a number of cents, the new frequency f2 is \[
f_2 = f_1 \times 2^{\mathrm{cents}/1200},
\] where “cents” is positive to go higher in pitch or negative to go lower in pitch.
For example, if we’re trying to play A4 and our electronic tuner says we’re flat by 30 cents, our actual frequency is \[
f = (440\,\mathrm{Hz})\times 2^{-30/1200} = (440\,\mathrm{Hz})\times(0.983) = 432\,\mathrm{Hz}
\]
Another example, say we’re trying to play C4 (262 Hz) and our measured frequency is 256 Hz. How far off are we in cents? \[
\frac{1200}{\log 2}\,\log\left(\frac{256\,\mathrm{Hz}}{262\,\mathrm{Hz}}\right) = -30\,\mathrm{cents}\text{ (flat)}
\]

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
MIDI pitch values
MIDI is a computer language for describing music. Pitch values for MIDI start with A4 = 69, and go up or down by one step for each key on the piano. In other words, 1 MIDI pitch unit = 100 cents.
To go from MIDI pitch p to frequency f do \[
f = (440\,\mathrm{Hz})\times 2^{(p-69)/12} = (8.175\,\mathrm{Hz})\times 2^{p/12}
\]
To go from frequency to MIDI pitch \[
p = \frac{12}{\log 2}\log\left(\frac{f}{8.175\,\mathrm{Hz}}\right)
= 39.86 \log\left(\frac{f}{8.175\,\mathrm{Hz}}\right)
\]

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Just intervals


The equal tempered scale is used for modern instruments because of its versatility, but it sacrifices perfect harmony. From our discussion of harmonic and inharmonic partials, we know that the best-sounding frequency combinations come as simple fractions.
These are called just intervals. Notice in the table that (1) these are not powers of \(2^{1/12}\) in frequency or (2) an even number of 100 cents.
This means that the equal-tempered scale of the piano is off by a tiny bit. This is the cost paid for being able to play music written in any key equally well.

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Pythagorean scales


If we try to build scales using just intonation, we have to make comprising about which intervals to preserve.
One of the earliest methods was the Pythagorean scale. This is made by choosing a starting pitch (e.g. A4 = 440 Hz) and going up or down in perfect fifths (3/2 ratio = 702 cents), then dropping or raising by octaves as needed to build the A4 major scale. This automatically includes the perfect fourths as well.
Extending this idea to all 12 tones is called “five-limit tuning”.




The root cause is that no number of perfect fifths add up to a whole number of octaves. Algebraically, there is no solution to the equation \[
(3/2)^x = 2^y,
\] where both x and y are whole numbers.

title: Lecture 13: Musical Intervals and Scales
text:
Just diatonic scale
If we use the same method as the Pythagorean scale but allow major and minor thirds, we get the just diatonic scale, which is the most harmonious 12-tone scale, in the sense of being closest to just for each note.
 
This works great for instruments with adjustable pitch (especially human voice or strings in most keys), but doesn’t work for things like pianos because it depends on picking one specific key in which the music is played (e.g. A-major starting from A4).

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Parts of a transverse wave




Transverse wave




Wavelength (λ) is the distance (measured in meters) between repetitions of the wave.
Amplitude (a) is the distance from the top of the wave (“crest”) to the middle. (Not crest to trough.)

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Wavelength, speed, and frequency
If we measure the speed of waves (e.g. using a high-speed camera), we can see a relationship between the speed (v), frequency (f), and wavelength (λ):
\[ v = f\lambda \]
This equation is true for all types of waves (sound, light, water, etc.)

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Standing wave
If we vibrate a string with certain special frequencies, the impulses travelling in each direction will overlap exactly and create a standing wave.





There will be points on the wave that do not move at all. These are called nodes (labeled by N on the drawing.)
There are also points where the amplitude is maximal. These are the anti-nodes (labeled A).
Each pair of adjacent nodes (or anti-nodes) is separated by half of a wavelength: λ/2.

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Resonant wavelengths


For a string clamped at both ends (like most stringed instruments), the rule is that the length of the string (L) must be a whole number of half-wavelengths (“loops”).
\[ L = n \frac{\lambda}{2}, \] where n is a whole number. Solving for wavelength we have \[ \lambda_n = \frac{2L}{n}. \]
Together these are called the resonant wavelengths (λn) of the string.




W&W Figure 11-2

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Resonant frequencies


If we combine the relationships between wavelength and frequency of a wave with the resonant wavelengths (λn), we get
\[
f_n = \frac{v}{\lambda_n} = n \frac{v}{2L} = nf_1.
\]
These are the resonant frequencies of the string. (Remember, n is a whole number: 1, 2, 3, etc.)
Notice that the fundamental frequency \(f_1=v/(2L)\) depends on two things:

the length of the string L
the speed on the string v.

We’ll come back to this.




W&W Figure 11-2

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Normal modes demo

https://phet.colorado.edu/en/simulations/normal-modes

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Perfectly plucked string
Which modes are excited when you bow, pluck, or hammer a string depend on where and how you move the string.
Each mode fn will have its own amplitude, just like in the simulation.
In the approximation that you pluck a string a small amount at a precise position x on the string (measured in meters), the amplitude of the nth mode, an is given by
\[
a_n = \sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L} \right),
\] where the sine function is evaluated in radians mode.

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Example calculation

Download the Excel file

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Choosing the harmonics


Because of the earlier equation, if you pluck a string at the antinodes and stop it at the nodes of a particular mode, you can select which harmonics get activated.
The mode drawing gives you a map about where to do this.
For example, if you stop a string exactly in the center and pluck it 1/4 of the way from the end, then gently release the stop, you’ll excite the 2nd harmonic (which is the octave).
Try this with a real instrument and see if you can do it.
(Bonus: on a fretted instrument, which frets line up with the nodes of certain modes?)




W&W Figure 11-2

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Changing the speed
The two ways we can adjust the fundamental mode of a string are by changing the length of the string or the speed of waves on the string.
The speed depends on two factors:

How much tension force F is on the string
How “heavy” the string is, where “heavy” is the mass density of the string m (e.g. kilograms per meter)

\[
v = \sqrt{\frac{F}{m}}
\]

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Mersenne’s Rules
Combining the speed equation with the mode equation gives Mersenne’s Rules
The fundamental frequency of a string is

Inverse to its length (L)
Proportional to the square root of the tension (F)
Inverse to its diameter (thickness)
Inverse to the square-root of its density

These are summarized by the equation
\[
f_1 = \frac{v}{2L} = \frac{1}{2L}\,\sqrt{\frac{F}{m}},
\] where rule 3 and 4 are combined as the mass per length m.

title: Lecture 10: Strings
text:
Example
A guitar string has length L=62.8 cm. One string has a mass per length of m=5.3 g/m and a tension of F=230 N. What is its frequency?

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Human ear



Outer ear - collects the sound as a propagates through the air

Pinna - the part of the ear on the outside (sound bucket) the funnels sound into the ear canal
Ear canal - impedance matches between the outside air and the ear drum

Middle ear - a set of 3 small bones act as levers to match the impedance between the ear drum and the oval window on the cochlea.
Inner ear - converts sound waves into nerve impulses (see next page)




Source: NIH

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Inner ear


The cochlea is the sound processing organ in the ear.

shaped like a long spiral containing a pair of fluid-filled tubes
between the tubes is the basilar membrane that sorts the sound waves by frequency
on the basilar membrane is the Organ of Corti that contains hair cells
the hair cells vibrate as the sound waves pass, stimulating the nerve cells to send signals to the brain




Long wavelength (low frequency) sound waves penetrate deeper into the cochlea than short wavelength (high frequency) waves. This is how the ear distiguishes pitch.

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
NIH video
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Journey_of_Sound_to_the_Brain.ogv

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Definitions of Intensity and Loudness

Intensity is a measure of how much sound energy passes through a surface in a certain amount of time (power per area).

This is an objective description of sound that we can measure with a machine (e.g. with a sound meter).

Loudness (or “volume”) is the human perception of sound intensity.

It is a subjective description of sound as heard by each listener.
Dynamics markings in a musical score (forte, piano, etc.) indicate loudness.
Because each listener may hear something different (e.g. because of age or medical condition), loudness is harder to describe precisely.
We’ll come back to loudness in the next chapter to talk about how an average adult human perceives intensity and the different factors that affect loudness.

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Energy, Power, and Intensity



Energy (E) is the capacity to do work. It is measured as joules (J), which the book also calls newton-meters (N m).
Power (P) is the rate at which energy is emitted or absorbed. Power is measured in watts (W), which is also joules per seconds.
Intensity (I) is the amount of power divided by the area it passes through, measured in watts per square-meter (W/m2).

\[P = IA\]



The power is represented by total number of arrows through each green area.
The intensity is the power divided by the area in each case.

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Example problem on intensity


A studio monitor outputs 2 W of sound power. The diameter of the speaker is 20 cm (about 8 inches). What is the sound intensity just next to the front of the speaker?
(Reminder: area of a circle is \(A=\pi d^2/4\) .)

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
We sense ratios, not differences
The sound intensities that humans can here vary from about 10-12 W/m2 to 1 W/m2, a factor of a trillion apart. The way our brain deals with great spreads like this is to measure ratios (fractions) rather than differences. (Also, this happens because of the way nerves activate.)
For example, we can sense when intensity doubles, but we can’t hear a whisper added to a jet engine.
Our brain divides and multiplies; it does not add or subtract.
Many of our senses use this type of compression:

Sound intensity, sound frequency (musical pitch)
Light intensity (but not color)
Touch (pressure)
Heat flow

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Decibels
To avoid writing very small and very large numbers (lots of zeros or decimals points) to represent quantities with a lot of dynamics range, engineers often use a logarithmic scale called decibels (dB) to express ratios.
The mathematical definition of decibels is
\[\text{decibels} = 10 \log\left(\frac{\text{measurement value}}{\text{reference value}}\right),\]
where “log” is the common (base-10) logarithm function found on a calculator.
If we’re given the decibel level and we need the measurement value, we solve the equation above to get
\[\text{measurement} = \text{reference value} \times 10^{\text{decibels}/10} \]

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Sound intensity levels (SIL)
The reference value for a decibel conversion is usually something agreed upon by the community. For example, when measuring sound intensity, the reference value is taken as the threshold of hearing for a typical adult human: I0 = 10-12 W/m2, giving us the following definition for Sound Intensity Level (SIL), measured in decibels:
\[S_{IL} = 10 \log(I/I_0)\]
or, flipping this around to solve for intensity:
\[I = I_0 \times 10^{S_{IL}/10},\]
where for sound, I0 = 10-12 W/m2 by definition.

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Rules of thumb for decibels
If you don’t have a calculator handy…

0 dB means the same
+3 dB is about twice as big
+5 dB is about three times as big
+10 dB means ten times bigger
+20 dB means 100 times bigger
The pattern +#0 dB is 1 with # zeros times bigger.
negative dBs means that factor smaller: -10 dB = ten times smaller.
adding two dB values is the same as multiplying their intensities.

Example: +13 dB = 10 dB + 3 dB = 10 × 2 = 20 times bigger.

subtracting two dB values is the same as dividing their intensities.

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Example problems: decibels

A typical speaking voice at a comfortable distance has an intensity level of 70 dB.

What is the intensity value (in W/m2)?
If two people talk at the same time, what is the combined intensity? combined SIL?

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Limits of human hearing


We’ll discuss the idea of loudness in detail next time, but one simple difference is that our experience of loudness depends on the frequency of the sound. Human ears work best around 1000 Hz. Our ears are less sensitive at higher and lower frequencies.
The red curve shows the minimum sound intensity that we can detect as a function of frequency. The blue curves are loudness values relative to the threshold.




Perceived intensity vs. frequency (Fletcher–Munson loudness curves)

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Dynamic Range
An important concept related to this that doesn’t show up in the textbook until the chapters on recording is dynamic range.
Dynamic Range is the ratio between the largest possible measurement and the smallest possible measurement.
So, the dynamic range of intensity for human hearing is about 1012 or 120 dB. By comparison, a 16-bit ADC on a digital recording deck has a dynamic range of 216 ≈ 65,000 or 48 dB.
The dynamic range of frequency for human hearing is about 20,000 Hz/20 Hz = 1,000 or 30 dB.

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Line levels


Audio recording equipment usually specifies intensity relative to the line level. Line level is the maximum signal that can pass through an electronic audio system without saturating (analog) or clipping (digital) the signal.
In this case the reference level is always higher than the sound level. When we convert this to decibels, we get a negative number. Roughly, the numbers come out to

0 dB = Maximum signal without distortion
-10 dB = an typical signal value
-45 dB = the lowest signal detectable

We’ll come back to the details later in the semester.




Amplifier gains and attenuation are usually specified in decibels.

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Intensity and distance


Intensity decreases as you move away from a source. If you were in completely empty space (no walls, floors, or ceiling), sound waves would come out in the shape of spheres.
Imagine blowing up a baloon with a sound source in the center of the baloon. The same amount of sound power (P) passes through the surface of the baloon regardless of how big it is, but the intensity will go down as the radius (r) of the baloon gets bigger.




The relationship between sound intensity and distance is called the inverse square law. Many laws of physics have this pattern: gravity, electricity, and light intensity, for example.




\[P = IA = I (4\pi r^2)\quad\rightarrow\quad I = \frac{P}{4\pi r^2}\]

title: Lecture 8: Sound Intensity and Hearing
text:
Example: Intensity


The Sumbergh Head foghorn in Shetland, UK, emits a tone of 120 Hz that can be heard 30 kilometers away. If the threshold of hearing at this frequency is SIL = 30 dB, what is the power of the foghorn? (Assume the sound waves come out as perfect spheres.)




Sumbergh Head foghorn

Most longitudinal waves are pressure waves


Sound waves in air are fluctuations in pressure.
These pressure fluctuations are tiny (a billionth to a thousandth of atmospheric pressure)!
We typically don’t measure the pressure directly, although speakers and microphones work on this principle.

In space no one can hear you scream.


Is this a true statement? Why?

Space has no air, so sound can’t travel.

title: Lecture 4: Longitudinal Waves and Sound Transmission
text:
Medium of transmission
Sound waves must travel through some kind of matter, which we call the medium of transmission.
For music, the medium is usually air, but sound also travels through

the solid body of musical instruments
the floors and walls
the structures of the human body (e.g. the parts of the ear)

Today we want to define a couple of terms describing how sound moves through matter: speed and impedance.

title: Lecture 4: Longitudinal Waves and Sound Transmission
text:
Speed of sound


The speed of sound in an object depends on many factors


the material
the environment (temperature, ambient pressure, humidity)
the shape of the object
the frequency of the sound (“dispersion”)

title: Lecture 4: Longitudinal Waves and Sound Transmission
text:
Waves carry information
We think of the speed of sound as telling us how quickly energy moves through a medium, but it also tells us how fast information travels.
We can demonstrate this will a high-speed camera…

title: Lecture 4: Longitudinal Waves and Sound Transmission
text:
Impedance
A second property of the medium or object that sound travels through is impedance.
Impedance (symbol: z, units: Pa m/s = Rayls) is a measure of how difficult it is for sound to move through a medium or object.
For bulk media (as opposed to a specific object), the impedance is related to the speed of sound and the density of the medium:
\[ z = \rho v, \]

z is the impedance (units of Pa s/m, also called “Rayls”)
ρ is the mass density of the medium (units of kg/m3)
v is the speed of sound in the medium (units of m/s)

title: Lecture 4: Longitudinal Waves and Sound Transmission
text:
Example: impedance of air
For example, air at room temperature (20°C) has a speed of v = 343 m/s and a density of ρ = 1.20 kg/m3. Therefore, the impedance of air at room temperature is z = ρv = 413 Pa m/s.

title: Lecture 4: Longitudinal Waves and Sound Transmission
text:
Sound at boundaries


When a sound wave strikes a boundary between different materials, the energy divides three ways:

some is transmitted through the boundary
some is reflected by the boundary
some may be absorbed, depending on the material

Impedance is the property that tells us about transmission and reflection.

title: Lecture 4: Longitudinal Waves and Sound Transmission
text:
Impedance matching


For sound to go efficiently from one object/medium to another, their impedances must be equal.

The bones of the human inner ear match the impedance of air to the impedance of the fluid inside the cochlea.
A car muffler works by intentionally mismatching the impedance of the exhaust pipe and the outside air.
In general, if sound is moving from impedance z1 to impedance z2, the fraction of transmitted sound energy will be \[ T = 1-\left(\frac{z_1-z_2}{z_1+z_2} \right)^2 \]





Human Ear





Muffler

title: Lecture 4: Longitudinal Waves and Sound Transmission
text:
Impedance matching video




https://youtu.be/AzZ7DjS4ti4. Part 2 (Human ear): https://youtu.be/geSDcollRos

title: Lecture 4: Longitudinal Waves and Sound Transmission
text:
Musical instruments


One of the main functions of a musical instrument is to match the impedance of the sound source (e.g. violin string, oboe reed, human vocal folds) to the outside air.
Consider the difference between an acoustic guitar and an electric guitar. Which one is impedance matched to the air?
A trumpet mute also works by changing the impedance of the trumpet, not by absorbing the sound.

title: Lecture 2: Sound
text:
Sound
We’re going to define sound from a scientific perspective as “mechanical vibrations transmitted through an elastic medium.”



“mechanical” meaning that objects are moving (gas molecules or wood molecules or etc.)
“vibrations” we’ll define later
“transmitted” meaning that something is travelling from one place to another
the “medium” is the substance the sound travels through
“elastic” means that the changes to the medium are temporary

title: Lecture 2: Sound
text:
Propagation of Sound


For music, sound mostly propagates through the air.

There are regions of compressed air that travel from the source to the listener.


Looking more closely, we see something like



Animated sound wave







Sound through air

title: Lecture 2: Sound
text:
Periodic motion

“Periodic” means repeating exactly over time.




The motion can be simple…



Simple periodic motion





… or complex



Complex periodic motion






as long as it repeats. Musical sound is mostly periodic.


The time between repetitions is the period, usually represented by T and measured in seconds.

title: Lecture 2: Sound
text:
Springs demo




https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/masses-and-springs/latest/masses-and-springs_en.html

title: Lecture 2: Sound
text:
Simple harmonic motion
The simplest type of periodic motion is called “simple harmonic motion”.
Physics has a lot of examples, some shown here:



Pendulum, Spring, Reed, Tuning Fork



To a good approximation, these all obey the same math.

title: Lecture 2: Sound
text:
Definitions


Period is the time between repetitions (T), measured in seconds (s)
Wavelength is the distance between repetitions (λ, Greek letter lambda), measured in meters (m)
Frequency is the number of waves per second (f), measured in “cycles per second” or Hertz (Hz)
Phase is how far along the repeating cycle is at one instant of time. We’ll come back to this later.

title: Lecture 2: Sound
text:
Sound wave demo




https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/waves-intro/latest/waves-intro_en.html

title: Lecture 2: Sound
text:
Frequency and period
Frequency (f) and period (T) have a simple relationship:
\[ f = \frac{1}{T} \qquad\mathrm{or}\qquad T = \frac{1}{f}. \]
Usually in music and acoustics we’re more interested in the frequency.

title: Lecture 2: Sound
text:
Example problem
The concert A used as the tuning note for most modern orchestras has a frequency of f = 440 Hz. What is the period of this sound?

The equation we need is
\[ T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{440\,\mathrm{Hz}} = 0.0023\,\mathrm{s} = 2.3\,\mathrm{ms}. \]


The period of a concert A is 2.3 milliseconds.

title: Lecture 11: Tubes
text:
Review: Resonances in strings


A string of length L that is clamped on both ends will resonate at the wavelengths
\[
\lambda_n = \frac{2L}{n}
\] with frequencies \[
f_n = \frac{v}{\lambda_n}=n\frac{v}{2L}= n\,\frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{F}{m}},
\] where v is the wave speed on the string,F is the tension force on the string,and m is the mass per length of the string.

title: Lecture 11: Tubes
text:
Open pipes


A pipe that is open on both ends has the same resonance structure as a clamped string: \[
\lambda_n = \frac{2L}{n}
\] with frequencies \[
f_n = \frac{v}{\lambda_n}=n\frac{v}{2L},
\] where v is now the speed of sound in air (about 350 m/s).
Note that the width of the pipe has very little effect on the frequency.




Pipe open on both ends



These curves show the air speed. Nodes are where the air does not move, and antinodes are where the air vibrates the most.

title: Lecture 11: Tubes
text:
Half-closed pipes


A pipe that is closed on one end will have a node at the closed end. For the fundamental mode, only a quarter of a wave fits inside the tube. \[
\lambda_1 = 4L \quad \rightarrow \quad f_1 = \frac{v}{4L}
\]
This means the fundamental frequency of a tube is halved when you close one end. Or in musical terms, a closed tube is one octave lower than an open tube of the same length.
Also, only odd harmonics give us the right combinations of nodes and antinodes: \[
f_n = n\,\frac{v}{4L}\quad\text{($n$ is odd)}
\]




Pipe open on both ends



These curves show the air speed. Nodes are where the air does not move, and antinodes are where the air vibrates the most.

title: Lecture 11: Tubes
text:
Selecting pitch


Because open ends of a tube give the location of anti-nodes in the vibration, wind instruments can change their pitch by opening holes in the side of the tube wherever antinodes are desired. This is also why the fingering for woodwinds can seem random: it’s not just changing the length, but adding interior antinodes as well.




Finger holes on a flute

title: Lecture 11: Tubes
text:
Speed of sound: factors
We’ve been casually passing around numbers for the speed of sound in air between 340 and 350 m/s.
The actual value of speed of sound in a gas depends on many factors:

the mass of the gas molecules
the molecular structure
the pressure of the gas
the temperature
if the gas is a mixture or not

title: Lecture 11: Tubes
text:
Speed of sound: important trends
The table in the book is basically useless because you need much more information to calculate the speed, but the basic trends are that speed increases when

ambient pressure increases
the temperature increases
the average molecular mass decreases

e.g. humidity of air increases (water weighs less than nitrogen or oxygen)


For dry air, the most relevant thing is the temperature dependence. A good approximation is
\[
v_\text{dry air} \approx (331.3 + 0.606\,T)\,\mathrm{\tfrac{m}{s}}
\] where the temperature T is in Celsius (e.g. T=20°C is room temperature).

title: Lecture 11: Tubes
section: Speed of Sound in other gases: Details
text:
Speed of Sound in other gases: Details


This page is bonus material.
A more detailed formula for the speed of sound in gas is given by
\[
v = \sqrt{\frac{K p}{d}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{KRT}{M}}
\]

K is adiabatic compressibility (about 1.4 for air)
p is gas pressure (101325 Pa)
d is gas density (in kg/m3)
T is temperature (in Kelvin, 293 K for room temp.)
R is molar gas constant (8.314 J/mol K)
M is molecular mass of the gas (in kg/mol)





Gas properties for common gases

title: Lecture 11: Tubes
text:
Turbulence creates vibrations
In flutes, recorders, whistles, and organ flue pipes, the air inside the tube starts vibrating because of turbulence, which is chaotic motion of the air. The frequencies in the turbulence that fail to match the resonances of the tube get suppressed.





Flue pipe (W&W Fig. 12-8)






Flute



Image Source: https://www.yorkshirecorpsofdrums.com/playflute3.html

title: Lecture 11: Tubes
text:
Reeded and brass instruments


The vibrations of the reeds in woodwinds and organ reed pipes adjust to match the partials of the pipes because of positive feedback. As the air in the pipe starts vibrating, the pulses come back to the mouthpiece at just the right time to push back on the reed, forcing it to only vibrate at the resonant frequencies.
The vibrations of a brass player’s lips work the same way: positive feedback from the air impulses forces the lips to vibrate resonantly with one of the partials of the instrument.




Clarinet mouthpiece





Feedback in a trumpet

Daily Quiz

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Prelude: Conservation of Energy
Energy can not be created or destroyed. It can only change forms.
For sound the two most relevant types of energy are

Energy of motion (e.g. vibrations, striking a bell, bowing a string)
Heat

An instrument can not put out more sound energy than the energy put into it.
The energy we use to sound an instrument (e.g. by plucking a string or buzzing a mouthpiece) goes somewhere.

Some goes into making the instrument vibrate.
Some goes into making the air vibrate.
And some is lost to heat.

How that energy is distributed depends on the resonances of the instrument.

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Definition of resonance
I like a slightly different definition of resonance than the textbook. The key is energy.
Resonance frequency is a frequency at which an object likes to intake, store, and emit energy.

The couplings describe how easy it is to add or subtract energy from the system. These depend on things like impedance matching.

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Demo videos

Mould resonance video: https://youtu.be/dihQuwrf9yQ
Mould Chladni video: https://youtu.be/CR_XL192wXw
Nigel Stanford Chladni: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFAcYruShow
Sante Fe Symphony Chladni: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9uEeADQN8Jo

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Waveform
A graph of sound pressure over time is called a waveform. Here is a waveform of a bell being struck:

The horizontal scale is time and the vertical scale is pressure.
If we zoom in we can see the individual oscillations



Ref: http://www.tonmeister.ca/wordpress/2019/08/19/dfts-part-3-the-math/

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Frequency spectrum


We can take the numerical data of the waveform and turn it into a frequency spectrum using a math technique called a Fourier transform.
The horizontal scale is frequency (plotted with logarithmic spacing) and the vertical scale is the sound intensity in decibels.
You can see two peaks. These are the resonant frequencies of this bell: 600 Hz and 1500 Hz.




The heights of these peaks tell us how much energy is being emitted at each frequency.
The widths of the peaks are inverse to the decay time of each resonance, which we can use to measure the coupling strengths and how much energy is lost to heat.


Ref: http://www.tonmeister.ca/wordpress/2019/08/19/dfts-part-3-the-math/

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Timbre
The combinations of resonant frequencies for an instrument determine its timbre.
 https://www.audiolabs-erlangen.de/resources/MIR/FMP/C1/C1S3_Timbre.html#Partials

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Energy in and out
Because resonance affect both energy in and energy out, we can also use resonance to capture sound.
Bass traps used in recording studios take advantage of this to ruin unwanted resonances of the room. The trap absorbs energy at the resonance frequency, turning it into heat.
Parasitic resonances are unwanted resonances that absorb sound you want and turn it into heat or into “bad” sound. (E.g. a rattling speaker cone in a cheap stereo.)
Microphones and speakers are designed to avoid resonances or to have very broad, flat resonance peaks, to avoid parasitic resonances.

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Waves add
When we have multiple sound sources, their waveforms add:

The fast oscillations are at the average frequency of the top two signals. The slow ripples are the “beat notes”. They occur at the difference of the two frequencies. This is an easy way to tell if two instruments are in tune with each other.
We’ll talk more about adding together waves in the next chapter.

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Beat demos
https://academo.org/demos/wave-interference-beat-frequency https://www.audiolabs-erlangen.de/resources/MIR/FMP/C2/C2S3_InterferenceBeating.html#Chirp-Experiment

title: Lecture 5: Resonance, Beats, and Doppler
text:
Doppler effect
Motion of a sound source or of the observer changes the observed frequency of sound. This is called the Doppler Effect.
    
If the source and observer are moving towards each other, the frequency that the observer hears goes up.
If the source and observer are moving away from each other, the frequency that the observer hears goes down.


Ref: https://www.flippingphysics.com/doppler-effect.html

Definition of Transverse Wave
A transverse wave has the motion of the medium moving perpendicular to the direction that the wave itself is traveling.

 


The archetypal transverse wave is a vibrating string.

title: Lecture 3: Transverse Waves
text:
Parts of a transverse wave




Transverse wave




Wavelength (λ) is the distance (measured in meters) between repetitions of the wave.
Amplitude (a) is the distance from the top of the wave (“crest”) to the middle. (Not crest to trough.)

title: Lecture 3: Transverse Waves
text:
Wavelength, speed, and frequency
If we measure the speed of waves (e.g. using a high-speed camera), we can see a relationship between the speed (v), frequency (f), and wavelength (λ):
\[ v = f\lambda \]
This equation is true for all types of waves (sound, light, water, etc.)

Example: A vibrating guitar string has a speed of v = 500 m/s and a wavelength of λ = 1.2 m.
What is the vibration frequency (f) of the string?


\[ v = f\lambda \quad\rightarrow\quad f = \frac{v}{\lambda} = \frac{500\,\mathrm{m/s}}{1.2\,\mathrm{m}} = 417\,\mathrm{Hz} \]

title: Lecture 3: Transverse Waves
text:
Standing wave
If we vibrate a string with certain special frequencies, the impulses travelling in each direction will overlap exactly and create a standing wave.





There will be points on the wave that do not move at all. These are called nodes (labeled by N on the drawing.)
There are also points where the amplitude is maximal. These are the anti-nodes (labeled A).
Each pair of adjacent nodes (or anti-nodes) is separated by half of a wavelength: λ/2.

title: Lecture 3: Transverse Waves
text:
Resonant wavelengths
The condition to have a standing wave depends on the details at the end of the wave. For the case of a string clamped at both ends (like most stringed instruments), the rule is that the length of the string (L) must be a whole number of half-wavelengths.
\[ L = n \frac{\lambda}{2}, \] where n is a whole number. Solving for wavelength we have \[ \lambda_n = \frac{2L}{n}. \]

When n = 1, we call this the fundamental wavelength of the string.
Higher n values give the harmonics or partials of the string.

Together these are called the resonant wavelengths (λn) of the string.

title: Lecture 3: Transverse Waves
text:
Resonant frequencies
If we combine the relationships between wavelength and frequency of a wave with the resonant wavelengths (λn), we get
\[
f_n = \frac{v}{\lambda_n} = n \frac{v}{2L}.
\]
These are the resonant frequencies of the string. (Remember, n is a whole number: 1, 2, 3, etc.)

When n = 1, we call this the fundamental frequency of the string.
Higher n values give the harmonics or partials of the string.

For example, if the fundamental frequency of a string is f1 = 440 Hz, then the first few harmonics are

f2 = 2 f1 = 880 Hz,   f3 = 3 f1 = 1320 Hz,   f4 = 4 f1 = 1760 Hz, etc.
